summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PARENTING – Where mother passed away from cancer – Where father allegedly engaged in serious persistent long term family violence against mother – Where mother’s wishes were for children to live with maternal grandmother – Where findings of serious verbal and physical family violence, including multiple punching and two acts of strangulation, by father against mother to which children were exposed: By consent children to live with maternal grandmother, who will exercise sole decision-making authority with consultation. FAMILY LAW – PARENTING – Long term supervision by family member – Where father poses an unacceptable risk of spending unsupervised time with children - Where maternal grandmother and Independent Children’s Lawyer propose long term supervision by paternal grandmother – Where paternal grandmother has shown a willingness to prioritise children’s safety over father’s interests – Where paid supervision would result in no-time - Where indefinite supervision by paternal grandmother is found to be preferable to no time – Various restraints and ancillary orders made.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

SMITH J:

These are oral reasons for decision in a final hearing in parenting proceedings to determine the parenting orders pursuant to Pt VII of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) which are in the best interests of two children, X born in 2019, age six, and Y born in 2022, age three, who I will refer to as ‘the children’.
The applicant father is Mr Winoc born in 1999, age 27. The father resides in Suburb S with his mother, the paternal grandmother, Mrs B Winoc born in 1975, age 50, and with the paternal grandfather, Mr C Winoc, as well as one of his brothers.
The children's mother was Ms D, born in 2000. She was diagnosed with cancer in September 2022 and tragically passed away in 2023, age 23. Her funeral took place in late 2023. X was three, and Y was one, at her passing.
The maternal grandmother, Ms Anatol, born in 1970, age 55, was substituted as and became the respondent after the mother's passing. The maternal grandmother lives with the mother's older sister, Ms E, born 1996, aged 29, and Ms E’s husband, Mr F, born in 1990, age 35, as well as with Ms E and Mr F’s three children, G, age three, and the twins, H and J, age one.
The mother has a half-sister, Ms K, who is referred to in the evidence. Ms K is the child of the mother's father, the paternal grandfather, Mr L, from a previous relationship. I will return to Mr L later.
I will refer to the father and the mother as ‘the parents’ and to the father and the maternal grandmother as ‘the parties’.
The mother and children effectively moved in with the maternal grandmother from September 2022, when the mother was first diagnosed. The children have continued to live with the maternal grandmother since then. Pursuant to consent interim orders made on 10 July 2024, the children presently live with the maternal grandmother and spend time with the father, which is supervised by the paternal grandmother, in week 1 on Wednesdays from 3 until 7 pm, and Sundays from 10 am to 4 pm, and in Week 2 from 10 am to 4 pm, Saturday and Sunday.
An Independent Children's Lawyer, or ICL, was appointed to represent the children's interests.

OUTLINE OF ORDERS

As this is an oral decision, and to avoid the further stress in the parties having to wait for an hour or two for a decision, I will indicate at this stage my basic findings, and the orders I propose to make, for reasons which I will then give.
At the commencement of the trial, the parties had competing live-with and sole long-term decision-making responsibility applications. At the conclusion of the evidence, and prior to oral submissions, the maternal grandmother and the ICL filed a joint final proposed minute of order, MFI 17, and the father filed an amended final proposed minute of order, MFI 18, in which the father agreed that the children should live with the maternal grandmother and that the maternal grandmother should have sole long-term decision-making authority.
The maternal grandmother and ICL proposed a consultation process around decision-making, which the maternal grandmother said in oral evidence she had been trying to give effect to, and which, in any event which, she was happy to continue. The father had a similar consultation proposal although in different terms.
I find that it is in the best interests of the children to live with the maternal grandmother and for the maternal grandmother to have sole long-term decision-making authority, as well as sole parental responsibility, subject to the consultation process which she and the ICL propose, and I will make those orders in effect by consent.
The parties also sensibly agreed, in substance, on many of the various procedural and other orders required to be made, including certain restraint, which I will go through later. These include a restraint on the maternal grandmother bringing the children into contact with the maternal grandfather, Mr L, for reasons to be explained.
This substantially narrowed the real issues in contention down to the question of the extent of time the children should spend with, and/or how often they should communicate with, the father, and to whether or not that time and communication should be supervised, and if so, whether it should be long-term supervision.
It was common ground that the children have a good and loving relationship with the father, and that it is in the children's best interest to maintain that relationship, and through that relationship, their relationship with the paternal family, to the extent it is safe to do so. Their safety with the father was the primary issue in this trial.
That primary issue arose from the question of whether or not the father had engaged in serious, persistent family violence throughout the parent’s relationship, directed at the mother, especially whilst engaging in significant cannabis and alcohol use, and if so, whether or not he has gained insight into and changed his behaviours, and subject to that, whether or not he poses an unacceptable risk of either, or both, physical and psychological harm to the children if he is permitted to spend unsupervised time with them.
In short, for reasons I will now go through, I find that the father did engage in serious long-term family violence against the mother, and that family violence occurred in the presence of the children. I find that despite certain protestations, the father has not, in fact, accepted responsibility for that violence. He has not gained true insight into the nature or gravity of his behaviours, or the impact of his behaviours on the mother and the children.
Consequently, I find that there is a real risk that if the children spend unsupervised time with the father, they may be subjected to serious physical and/or psychological family violence, and that consequently, balancing the issues, the father does pose an unacceptable risk to the children of their spending unsupervised time with him.
The child court expert, who I will come to, indicated that on those findings, this could be a no-time case, and it certainly would not be unusual or inconsistent with prior decisions of this Court for a no-time order to be made, given the extent of the family violence, which I will come to.
However, the ICL and the maternal grandmother, the latter showing, I find, enormous child focus, proposed, instead of a no-time order, or time once a month for a couple of hours as the child court expert also raised as a possibility, that there should be regular time spent by the children with the father every alternate Sunday for six hours on the basis, to make it practical, that it would be supervised by the paternal grandmother.
I raised with the parties, and am cognisant of the issues of familial supervision in a case involving serious risks. I note the issues articulated by the Full Court in B & B [1993] FamCA 143; (1993) FLC 92-357, under the heading ‘Who Should Supervise the Access’.
However, I note that the proposal from the maternal grandmother and the ICL, and as I will come to, the paternal grandfather has previously gone so far as to take the father to the ground to stop him from doing something rash, and that he did that in the presence of and with the support of the paternal grandmother, who gave evidence about having witnessed the paternal grandfather take the father to the ground to stop him going to the maternal grandmother’s.
Where both the ICL and the maternal grandmother are satisfied that the paternal grandmother is aware of the severity of the risks, and will act to put the children's interests first, and where in reality, if there had to be paid supervision, it would be effectively a no time order, I am satisfied that this is one of those relatively rare cases where it is appropriate in a case of serious family violence to have a family member supervisor. The paternal grandmother has been supervising, and has previously given an undertaking, and has shown a willingness to put the children's interests above the interests of her son the father. She will be required to give another supervisor’s undertaking.
I also make this long-term supervision order, having raised with the parties and having in mind the warnings about the potential issues, again, from B & B, are cited through Slater & Light [2013] FamCAFC 4; (2013) 48 Fam LR 573, Champness & Hanson [2009] FLC 93-47, and a line of cases following.
I make the order on the basis that, although indefinite supervision is highly undesirable, particularly given how young the children are, in circumstances where the alternative order would be the most severe order, which would be a no time and no communication order, then on balance, applying the relevant principles, it is better that the children have this imperfect order of long-term, regular time supervised by another family member, rather than a no time order or a once every couple of months order.
As I said, the various procedural orders were largely agreed. There will be a limited order in respect of communication because I think that that will create issues that outweigh it’s benefit, and I will come to the orders in more detail later.
However, just so the parties are aware, in effect, it will be the time as proposed by the maternal grandmother and the ICL at the end of the trial.

THE TRIAL

The trial commenced on 19 January 2026 with each party represented, including by counsel, and ran for five days to 23 January 2026.
The applicant father read or relied upon:
(1) His own affidavit filed on 19 January 2026;

(2) The paternal grandmother's affidavit filed on 19 January 2026;

(3) The Amended Application for Final Orders filed on 30 January 2025;

(4) His case outline filed on 16 January 2026;

(5) His final proposed orders provided 23 January 2026

The respondent maternal grandmother read or relied upon
(1) Her affidavit filed on 5 January 2025;

(2) The affidavit of Ms E filed on 5 December 2025;

(3) The supplementary affidavit of Ms E filed on 19 January 2026;

(4) The affidavit of Mr F filed on 5 December 2025;

(5) The Further Amended Response filed on 30 January 2025;

(6) Case outline and supporting documents filed on 12 January 2026;

(7) The final proposed minute of order provided with the ICL on 23 January 2026

The ICL relied upon:
(1) The case outline filed 15 January 2026;

(2) The Child Impact Report of Dr M dated 11 June 2024;

(3) Family Report of the Child Court Expert Ms N of 18 November 2024;

(4) A final proposed minute of order provided on 23 January 2026, jointly with the maternal grandmother.

The father, the paternal grandmother, the maternal grandmother, the maternal aunt, the maternal uncle and the child court expert Ms N were required for cross-examination.

NARROWING OF ISSUES

Due to the narrowing of the major issues to time and communication with the father at the end of the trial, the parties sensibly agreed that much of the affidavit and oral evidence led at trial not be considered.
I agree, noting that much of it no longer goes to a fact in issue and is therefore not relevant. That applies to much of the cross-examination of the maternal grandmother, which sought to identify deficits in her parenting capacity and household which might cause the Court to favour the father’s household, absent findings of serious family violence, and also cross-examination of the paternal grandmother, who was similarly cross-examined on her household for similar reasons, but who, I note, the maternal grandmother and ICL ultimately propose as a suitable person to supervise the father’s time with the children. Similarly, much of the evidence in cross-examination of Ms E and her husband Mr F falls away.

BACKGROUND

The father and mother met at V School. The father was in the year above the mother. They commenced a boyfriend/girlfriend relationship in about late 2015 when they were about 15 and 16 years old, in years 9 and 10. From this time, the father says they commenced spending time at the maternal grandmother’s house and that the mother would spend time at his house.
The mother had a significant mental health history due to, it appears, multiple sexual assaults, including allegedly by the maternal grandfather Mr L.
By 2019, the parents had left school. They decided to have a child and conceived X. The father, despite his criticisms of the maternal grandmother, said they decided to live with the maternal grandmother as his three brothers then aged 23, 17 and 13 were living with his parents. The parents later moved to their own property and conceived and had Y. The parents never married.
The mother was diagnosed with cancer in September 2022 and moved with the children to the maternal grandmother’s house, where they stayed until her passing in 2023.
The precise status of the parent’s relationship at September 2022 is unclear, but no finding needs to be made.
One of the major factual disputes that also falls away is how much time the children spent with the father, and the extent of the maternal grandmother’s willingness to facilitate time during the period September 2022 and the mother’s passing in 2023, including who in each extended family did and said what hurtful things.
The father commenced these proceedings on 11 October 2023 seeking short service and orders that he have sole parental responsibility, the children live with him, that they be delivered to him that day, spend time with the mother as agreed or every second day for at least two hours, and requiring the maternal grandmother to not be within 100 metres of him. He also sought orders in the alternative.
The documents were unfortunately served in 2023, [...] as the mother died. Whilst this caused an extreme emotional response in the mother’s family, and the father was severely criticised for this, in the context where the mother was dying and the children were with the maternal grandmother, who did not have any parental responsibility, and where the maternal grandmother was unwilling, for good reason at the time, I find, to give the children to the father, and where the mother’s life expectancy was not known and where the father could not know she would die [...], I find it was not unreasonable for the father to accept legal advice that it was appropriate to commence legal proceedings in the Court to determine the children’s future and to serve the documents. However, the service of the documents on this day greatly inflamed the already high conflict between the parties.
In late 2023, the maternal grandmother was joined to proceedings as the second respondent. On 8 November 2023, interim orders were made for the maternal grandmother to have sole parental responsibility for the children and for the children to live with her. It was ordered that the children spend weekend time with the father, with such time to be professionally supervised and to only commence after the father had engaged with a medical practitioner regarding the child Y’s nut allergy. At this time, the mother was removed as a party.
By consent, interim orders were made on 22 November 2023 varying the father’s time with the children so that he was to spend two hours each week for a period of six weeks with the children on days and times as nominated by a supervision centre.
A child impact report was prepared by Dr M dated 11 June 2024.[1]
The parties engaged in a family dispute resolution conference on 10 July 2024, which resulted in the consent interim orders already discussed.
A Family Report from panel child court expert Ms N was ordered in July 2024. Interviews were held on 29 October 2024 and the report dated 18 November 2024 was released the following day.[2]
On 31 January 2025, the matter was transferred to Division 1.
On 27 February 2025, orders were made appointing an ICL.
On 5 November 2025, orders were made varying the restrictions preventing the maternal grandmother from permitting the children to spend time with Mr L to allow them to attend the wedding of Ms E and Mr F.
On 7 November 2025, the matter was listed for final hearing to commence on 19 January 2026.
On 5 December 2025, orders were made for further testing of the father in respect of substances, and on 19 January 2026, the matter came on for final hearing.

FATHER'S ALLEGED FAMILY VIOLENCE AGAINST THE MOTHER

It is alleged that the father was physically and verbally abusive towards the mother throughout the course of their relationship. The maternal grandmother says that the father would push and hit the mother[3], become violent if the mother told him not to smoke[4], and kick the mother out of their house.[5] She also alleges that the father pushed the mother to the ground in June 2020, and says the mother did not take action as she was worried about the children.[6] She said the police were involved in October 2020 when the mother would not let the father inside of the home, and in November 2020, when a bystander called the police after overhearing an argument between them, and in December 2021 when the mother and X were dropped off at the side of the road.[7]
The maternal grandmother raised concerns about the father's capacity to manage the children's moods and behaviours due to his temper and emotional dysregulation, although she acknowledged that recently, this does not appear to have been an issue in terms of time with the children.[8]
Ms E said that in January 2019 she had a conversation with the mother where the mother said that she and the father were arguing in a car over how much cannabis he smoked. I will come to the January 2019 incident later.

MOTHER’S HISTORY OF MENTAL HEALTH

The mother had a significant mental health history as a teenager. The father alleges in his trial affidavit at about [10] to [20] that when at school he was told by friends, the mother “was having significant issues with her mental health and that there were issues with her family”[9] and that later the mother “informed me that she had difficulties in her relationship with her mother”[10] and had not spent time with the maternal grandfather for a couple of years.
He says that he was aware that the mother alleged that the maternal grandfather had sexually abused her,[11] and that in early 2016, the mother was admitted to the W Hospital following self-harm injuries,[12] and he said that the mother had told him she was trying to alleviate the pain that she was feeling with her mother, ie the maternal grandmother, and also that the maternal grandmother would not come and visit the mother as, he says, the maternal grandmother thought the mother was trying to create dramas.[13]
In cross-examination, the father agreed that the effect of his evidence was that the mother was self-harming to get the maternal grandmother's attention.

CHILD AND ADOLESCENT MENTAL HEALTH SERVICE REPORT 29 JUNE 2017

The father was taken through the report of Mr O of 29 June 2017 in relation to the mother. That report recorded, amongst other matters:[14]
[Ms D] (17-[...] yrs) self harmed (cutting) after an argument with her boyfriend when he told her to go home and cut herself deeply. She called her mother who took her to [DD Hospital Clinic] and then [EE Hospital] with a plan for admission to [FF Private Hospital] which she declined.

...

She has a strained relationship with her father who she rarely sees; he allegedly sexually abused her.

[Ms D] was raped by some boys in 2014 and said she did not proceed with steps offered by the Police for fear of ostracism at school.

...

She has had 7 admissions to [FF Private Hospital] since 2013, most recently in [late] 2016 following an attempted hanging, and has presented at ED numerous times with self harming/parasuicidal behaviours. She has had extensive service from [GG Mental Health Service]. Mother attended the [HH Women’s Health service].

...

She has been in a relationship for 2 years, with a boy who has recently been violent to her and who tells her to harm herself whenever they argue. She is ambivalent about leaving, though others tell her not to stay. Safety was discussed as well as the idea of him getting support for change.
In cross-examination, the father accepted he was the boyfriend referred to, knew that the mother had been raped by some boys at school, but did not report it, and was aware of her allegations about her father. He, however, denied being violent, and specifically denied that he had told the mother to cut herself.

19 JANUARY 2019: CONVERSATION BETWEEN PARENTS PRIOR ABOUT STRAGULATION EVENT

The audio/visual document, exhibit R7, is difficult to see. It is primarily relevant as audio evidence. The father did not dispute that the conversation recorded by the mother was a conversation between him and the mother. There is no agreed transcript, however, much or most of the argument between the parents is clear.
There was a long argument between the parents about the father's drug use. At one point, the father says: “I can’t do this anymore” to which the mother replies “then stop the drugs” and the father says “no, it’s you that I have to stop”.
He appears to be saying that the drugs have always been a part of his life, and when the mother says that he chose her over drugs, he says he did not. After discussion about his cannabis use, during which both of them are shouting at each other, significantly the mother says “after you strangled me, after all these things” to which the father replied “beforehand I strangled you”
The context makes it difficult to understand when or why the father strangled the mother, but I am satisfied that they are referring to a prior argument, and to the father having strangled the mother on an occasion prior to this conversation.
At the end of the argument, the father screams and appears to be pounding something, perhaps the steering wheel or dashboard, in frustration.
The date of the argument was not stated in or included visually on the audio/visual content of the document, which is the exhibit.
However, there was evidence filed at the commencement of the trial in the supplementary affidavit of Ms E, which the father was aware of, indicating that the electronic file had given a date of 19 January 2019.
In cross-examination, the father accepted that he referred to having strangled the mother in the audio tape, and was directed to the evidence indicating the date of the conversation was 19 January 2019. It was put to him that, prior to 19 January 2019, he had strangled the mother, to which the father did not deny, but said “I can’t recall but I don’t know if I did”
I will deal with the date of the recording now. The mother's sister, Ms E, provided the supplementary affidavit the first day of trial, 19 January 2026. That affidavit identified this audio/video as having been identified on the mother’s iPad, from which it was recovered electronically, as being dated 8.34 pm on 19 January 2019.
This was one of a number of audio and visual documents, including some I will come to later, recovered from the mother's iPad and iPhone, which she provided together with her password to the maternal grandmother just prior to her passing.
The mother's iPad and iPhone were called for by the father at trial, and were produced by the maternal grandmother with passwords to allow the father's legal representatives to check the provenance of the electronic documents and their saved dates as identified on the original devices left by the mother to the maternal grandmother.
Ms E was subsequently cross-examined on a variety of matters in her trial affidavit, most of which, as I have indicated, were ultimately not relevant, but some of which were, and which I will come to, but Ms E was not cross-examined on the veracity of her evidence as to what she found on the iPad, nor was it suggested to her that the electronic dates recorded on the iPad or iPhone for this exhibit had been interfered with or might be wrong.
It was not ultimately submitted to me in closing submissions that this conversation did not happen on 19 January 2019.
I am therefore comfortably satisfied that this was a recording of a conversation between the mother and the father on 19 January 2019, so that the reference to the father strangling the mother was to an event of strangulation prior to that date.
That is significant, as I will come to, because the father was convicted in respect of a subsequent strangulation event.

NOVEMBER 2019: FATHER ALLEGEDLY SMOKING CANNIBIS

The police record, in exhibit R14, says that:[15]
About 8pm on the [...] of November 2019 police were patrolling the [Suburb S] area. Police have sighted a [redacted] registration [redacted] parked in the corner of the dirt car park of the sporting grounds. Police approached the vehicle and have sighted a bag on the passenger seat with a bong in it. Police could also smell cannabis in the vehicle. Police asked the male seated in the drivers seat to step out of the vehicle. The male complied and police sighted a small plastic container containing cannabis in the drivers foot well. Police spoke with the male and he admitted to smoking a cone prior to police attending. He stated he paid $10 for a small amount. The male consented to be dealt with by way of a cannabis caution 130613. He has no prior criminal history.
The father accepted that he had a bong and cannabis but denied he told police he had just smoked a cone. I find the father's denial inherently improbable and unreliable, and note that this is one of many findings suggesting the father is a witness of poor credit. I prefer the contemporaneous, albeit hearsay record, of the minor event of the cannabis caution. This is one of many pieces of evidence concerning the father's cannabis use, including his own discussions on the tape, which I have just referred to.

JUNE 2020: FATHER ALLEGEDLY PUSHES MOTHER OVER

The maternal grandmother gave evidence in her affidavit that: [16]
On or about [...] June 2020 [Ms D] rang me and told me that [Mr Winoc] had pushed her to the ground. [Ms D] explained what happened::
[Ms D] said, "I threw him out mum, I got his clothes and I threw them out, he'd been out smoking and drinking again. "

I said, "are you okay? "

[Ms D] said, "no, he hurt me, he pushed me over twice. "
I then rang the police. Police said to me that they would, "send someone out." I never heard back from police after that. [Ms D] later told me that she spoke to police. She said to me,
"I didn't want to take it any further because I was worried what might happen to the kids"
The father's evidence on this issue was set out in his affidavit at paragraph 45:[17]
However, on one particular occasion [Ms D] and I had an argument concerning me leaving the home to go and spend time with a friend. On this occasion, I indicated to [Ms D] that I was going to go around to my friend's home to discuss a short holiday that I had planned with a group of my friends. In the course of getting ready to go to my friend's house, [Ms D] became upset and distressed that I was "leaving her" and so she proceeded to go into my wardrobe and grab a large amount of my clothes and proceeded to throw them on the front lawn and she told me not to come back. [Ms D] then contacted her Mother, but her Mother was at her boyfriend ' s house. [Ms D’s] mother then called the Police to attend upon [Ms D] and sought to have an Apprehended Domestic Violence Order taken out for [Ms D’s] protection against me. Prior to the Police arriving, [Ms D] had called my Mother and my Mother came over to be with [Ms D]. Upon my Mother arriving, she witnessed [Ms D] laughing on the lounge with one of her friends. My Mother stayed with [Ms D] until the Police arrived. The Police did not place any AVO on me.
The police record, exhibit R9, sets out the mother's complaint.[18]
Police arrived at [redacted] at 7:20pm on [...]/06/2020 and spoke to the pinop in relation to the incident. The pinop stated to police that at about 6pm on the said date she entered into a verbal argument with the defendant due to the fact the defendant wanted to go out drinking with his mates and she didn't want him to because she was scared that he may drink drive. The argument continued to the extend where the pinop has thrown a bag of the defendants clothes outside the front door of the premises.

The defendant has reacted to this by pushing the pinop with his hands to her body through the lounge room at the front of the house to the back door kitchen area. A further verbal argument has ensued and the defendant has pushed the victim to her upper body resulting in the pinop falling to the ground in the lounge room area.

Police requested the pinop provide a domestic violence evidence in chief statement in relation to the above incident, yet she declined. The pinop was asked several further times to provide police with a dvec yet she stated she would not. The victim did not have any visible injuries from the assault, yet stated she had a headache. The victim stated she was scared that if the defendant came back to the premises he would further assault her.

Due to the above disclosure to police all captured on body worn police video police are of the strong opinion that the pinop would benefit from a apprehended domestic violence order protecting her from the defendant.

To note, the young child was upstairs asleep whilst the incident was taking place.
In cross-examination, the father denied that he had pushed the mother through the house or that she had fallen to the ground.

JUNE 2020: SERVICE OF ADVO

The service of the ADVO in June 2020 was recorded in the COPS event, exhibit R17.[19]
[...]06/2020

Person named has recently separated from his long term partner, the couple have a [...] infant as a result of their relationship.

At 10.30am on Tuesday the POI was served an AVO at [Z] Police Station for a DV incident on the [...]06/2020.

The POI was shocked to be served the AVO and left the station angry.

About 11.12am the POI has sent a text message to [redacted] stating "Fucking will always love you guys Dont ever forget that thankyou for being there more than anyone else ever could.

This message has then been passes on to the PN's mother [redacted] whom has then attempted to contact the PN a number of times via telephone and text with no response.

[redacted] attended [Z] Police Station with this information. Whist at the police station police have called the PN on his mobile [redacted]. Police have spoken with the PN over the phone. The PN was clearly upset. He would not offer police his location and told police to tell his mother he would call her later. The PN then hung up on police.

Seconds later the PN has sent a text to his mother stating "Just go home fuck them I love you. I just can be here any more"

Police have then circulated a KLO4 the PN's vehicle.

About 2.20pm Further information was received from [redacted]. The PN had been communicating with [redacted] and sent a number of text messages. One of those messages was of the side of a cliff face (bushes and water seen in background) stating "I'm g man"

A further text states "I dont know [redacted] I got police looking for me Im just over it man I want to be able to close my eyes and not have to worry about shit.

About 2.40pm the PN sent further messages to [redacted] stating "Ill pop into [AA Store] later grab some clothes then go to [BB Real Estate Agency] to get me off the lease"

Sgt Burton made contact with [BB Real Estate Agency]. 3.10pm. [Redacted].

PN located at [redacted]. The PN was agitated however insited that he was fine. Based on the text messages the PN had sent to family and friends the PN was scheduled – Section 22. The PN was conveyed by ambulance to [CC Mental Health facility]. Police escort was not required.

In cross-examination, the father said there was an incident, and he was taken to the CC Mental Health facility, but he did not recall if he was scheduled.
On the issue of the father being scheduled, I prefer the contemporaneous police record.

12 MARCH 2021: FATHER ALLEGEDLY STRIKES MOTHER IN THE FACE

The maternal grandmother gave evidence that:[20]
In April or May 2021, I recall a conversation between myself and [Ms D] to the following effect:
[Ms D] said, "[Mr Winoc] has been violent with me a few times ... he slapped me across the face and I took a photo of the bruise. "

I said, "when did this happen?"

[Ms D] said, "a couple of months ago. "

I said, " that's not on , he can't do that, [Ms D] you've gotta stop this. "
I have not been able to find a copy of the photo to which [Ms D] referred. However, once the court made orders for final hearing I looked for the photo and in the course of searching for that photo I discovered a video on [Ms D’s] iPhone dated 12 March 2021 along with other videos to which I refer later.
The video of 12 March 2021 may have been what [Ms D] was referring to when she told me about the photo. I found the video in November 2025 and I sent it to my solicitor. The video shows [Ms D] looking in a mirror and lifting up her lip to show a bloodied bruise. In the video [Ms D] says,

"so I have a lump that actually split, like I can't get that blood off my lip and then underneath it he hit so hard it's like blistered."
The video is exhibit R28.
The audio recording of the exhibit is substantially as recorded by the maternal grandmother in her affidavit, although it appears to say, "That's actually split", with an “s” at the end of the word, "That."
The video shows the mother holding her lip to the camera showing a split lip with a distinctive red spot and a red bump on the gum line above one of the top centre teeth. It appears to me to be consistent with the mother having been struck.
In terms of the date of the video, this was also identified from the mother's device which was provided at trial as I’ve already discussed, and the date is as indicated on the electronic record.
Ultimately, no challenge was taken in submissions to the provenance of the document, to the fact that it showed the mother, or what she was saying, nor to the fact that the electronic record of the date was 12 March 2021.
When asked about the video in cross-examination, the father denied that he had been the person who hit the mother and who caused those injuries.

JULY 2021: ASSAULTS AND CHOKING

The maternal grandmother gave evidence at paragraphs [85] to [90], that in October or November 2022, the mother decided to make a police report concerning an assault that had occurred in July 2021.

The three videos showing the events relied upon, shown by the mother to the maternal grandmother and her sister, and then provided by the mother to police, were tendered before me as exhibits R4, R5 and R6.
The video evidence is substantially as reported by police when drafting the narrative referred to, and it is clear that they had copies of that video when speaking with the mother and drafting that narrative.
Consistent with the process of trial, I will refer to the: "punching video", the "skateboard video", and the "choking video”.
The punching video shows the father stepping into and punching the mother. The speed and apparent force of the punch shown in the video is consistent with it striking with sufficient force to establish the mother's statement to police that the punch caused her to, "to feel immediate pain in the abdominal area." It appears to be a full force punch. X was present.
The skateboard video shows the father grabbing the skateboard with both hands, raising it above and behind his head and making a movement forward which would, I find consistent with the conviction, have given a reasonable person in the mother's position a momentary belief that the father was about to hit her in a way which would, if that had occurred, have been expected to cause very significant physical injury. In that context, a reasonable person in the mother's position would have experienced real fear and apprehension for personal safety, making out the assault charge. X was present.
The choking video shows X held in the mother's arms, looking distressed and crying. The father's hand then clearly grabs the mother by the throat directly under the chin. The father's teeth are bared and his face is contorted in a grimace. The mother can faintly be heard to gasp for air as the father's hand releases her throat, consistent with the mother's statement to police that the choke hold "made her lose her breath for a moment."
The father was shown these videos in Court and accepted they showed him assaulting and battering the mother. It was not suggested that, contrary to the police statement, the mother had consented to any of the punch, choking or assault with a skateboard.
Significantly, as I will come to, the father was not only aware of his conduct because he was present, but was aware of the existence of these videos which he saw at the time he pleaded guilty, and was aware that the maternal grandmother had them. This is particularly relevant given his statements to the child court expert, which I will come to, that there was no physical violence at any stage during the relationship, an extraordinary statement to make in circumstances not only where there had been, but where he was aware that there was video evidence showing the violence and a record of criminal convictions by way of pleas of guilty.

JULY 2021: FATHER TAKEN TO GROUND BY PATERNAL GRANDFATHER

Having violently assaulted the mother, the father then decided to go to the maternal grandmother's house to confront her that same evening.
The police records in exhibit R16 were shown to the father[23]
[Mr Winoc] had recently separated from his long time girlfriend [Ms D] and wanted to confront [Ms D’s] parents as he believed they were the cause of the breakup. [Mr Winoc] told his mother what he was going to do and he headed to [...] to confront them. Before [Mr Winoc] reached [Ms D’s] parents home [Mr Winoc] was stopped by his father [Mr C Winoc]. Both [Mr C Winoc] and [Mr Winoc] got into a verbal argument and [Mr C Winoc] took [Mr Winoc] to the ground and held him there until he calmed down. Once [Mr Winoc] was calm both [Mr C Winoc] and [Mr Winoc] spoke about what would off happened if [Mr Winoc] reached [Ms D’s] parents home. Both settled their differences prior to police arrival.
In cross-examination, the father said he agreed that his father, Mr C Winoc, had stopped him, and that they had a verbal argument. When it was put to the father that the paternal grandfather had taken him to the ground, he said he "did not recall". When challenged on the fact that he said he could not recall what must have been a significant event of his own father having to take him to the ground, he said he could not recall most of what had happened due to stress.
There is no doubt that this event occurred as recorded by police, because in her oral evidence the paternal grandmother, when called in the father's case, was asked about the event and confirmed that she was present on the occasion when her husband, the paternal grandfather, had to take the father to the ground to stop him from going to the maternal grandmother's house.
It was put to the father, as a general proposition later, that his memory was very selective, and that when he was asked a question the honest answer to which would not assist his case, or would show him in a bad light, he repeatedly chose to say he did not recall. The father denied that.
Given the father was able to remember everything but the significant event of being taken to the ground by his own father, I find this was one of a number of examples where, when faced with evidence he did not like, the father chose to say he did not recall rather than admitting the truth.
As I have previously indicated, the willingness of the paternal grandparents to take significant action, including actually physically restraining the father to stop him doing something foolish or dangerous, is one of the reasons why both the maternal grandmother, the ICL, and I, are satisfied that the paternal grandmother is a safe person who will prioritise the children's safety and best interests over the father's interests, and therefore is a safe and appropriate person to supervise.

In his trial affidavit, the father said of the assault and trial, which resulted in convictions on his plea, noting that in paragraph 65, he is referring to November 2022:

The father later said, at [87] to [90] of the father's trial affidavit:

On the day of the hearing, somehow [Ms D] was able to appear, however I wanted to avoid her having to be placed under the stress of the Court hearing.

I note that the Fact Sheet was amended to reflect the generally agreed upon evidence.
It is common ground that the father did not enter a plea of guilty until the final hearing of the criminal charges.[26]
The convictions are as set out in the father's criminal record, exhibit ICL 3.
The father agreed in oral evidence that he had told the child court expert that he pleaded guilty as recorded by the expert at [27]. The father denied that in that statement to the child court expert that he was suggesting he was not in fact guilty by saying that.
In cross-examination on paragraphs [65] to [66] of his trial affidavit, the father maintained his evidence that the mother did not want to involve police, and that the police involvement was due to the mother's family seeking to exclude his involvement with the children. He maintained in oral evidence his statements at [58] to [59] of his affidavit, that one of the mother's final wishes was to marry him.
I note that whilst it is clear that the mother's family encouraged and supported her in making the police reports, the father's suggestion that the mother's final wish was to marry him, and that she was somehow forced or coerced or pressured by her family to go to police is inconsistent with the mother's actual conduct in choosing to go and live with her mother, to take the children with her, to show the video to her mother and half-sister which they would not otherwise have been aware of, to go to police and to make a statement, and to turn up at Court despite being extremely ill in order to make sure that the father was convicted, and in later giving a statement to a solicitor in family law proceedings, or in anticipation of family law proceedings, which I will come to, critical of the father.
In cross-examination on [87] to [90] of his affidavit, the father maintained he wanted to avoid the mother being placed under stress of a court case and therefore pleaded guilty.[27]
When cross-examined further about the opportunity to plead guilty prior to the trial date, the father says he could not recall whether he had a chance to plead guilty as he was "also dealing with the family court" and then "doing what my solicitor instructed me to". He agreed that the mother had to turn up to give evidence, and that he was surprised she turned up given how sick she was, but denied he waited till then to plead guilty in the hope she would not turn up and the charges would therefore be dismissed. I found this evidence to be entirely unpersuasive.
When asked by the court "the main reason he pleaded guilty", the father said it was, "because I had done something horrible and regretted it and did not want her to sit in the courtroom".
This was a clear and sensible statement of contrition. Reliance was placed on this statement in closing oral submissions for the father. The difficulty, as I shall come to, is that this statement was made during oral evidence, and in the face of overwhelming evidence having been put to the father in cross examination, and in the context of the father continuing to deny most of the alleged family violence which, as I will come to, I find occurred. In that context limited if any weight can be given to this late statement of contrition, limited to the one event already established to have occurred.

SUBSEQUENT EVENTS AND OTHER ISSUES

The father's alcohol and cannabis use, and his mental health, which may or may not be causally related to what I find is his history of family violence towards the mother, touched on already, were also matters at issue in trial, as was the father's general parenting capacity.
The maternal grandmother alleges that the father has engaged in constant drug use, that she engaged in various conversations with the mother in October 2022 and December 2022,[29] where the mother disclosed she was worried about the father smoking cannabis, and that she did not want the children to be with the father.[30]
Ms E gave evidence that when she visited the parents, she would often observe the father going into the laundry, that smoke would drift from the laundry, and she would smell cannabis when the father returned.[31]

The father says he has not smoked cannabis since he was in high school, and he has returned multiple negative drug screens recently.[33]
On general parenting issues and capacity, the maternal grandmother alleges that the father has been inconsistent and unreliable with his care of the children, often failing to show up to agreed pick-ups, changing his mind about spending time with the children, and saying he would leave the mother to look after the children on her own.[34]
The father says he has completed the Triple P Parenting program. He is learning about being a single parent,[35] and his case is that he intends to leave his parents’ home and establish his own home, but his parents will provide support.

...whilst the father acknowledged that he is still learning how to be an effective parent, he appears to be taking positive steps to gain this knowledge and appears to have strong supports around him to assist him with this.

RECOMMENDATIONS

Based on the information gathered in this assessment and unless evidentiary material should indicate otherwise, the following is recommended:
Consideration be given for Mr [Winoc] to have decision-making responsibility with Ms [Anatol] for the children until such time as the children are in the full-time care of Mr [Winoc].
It is recommended that Mr [Winoc] complete the Men’s Behaviour Change program and that the current spend time arrangement remain in place until he has completed the program.
It is recommended that Mr [Winoc] complete a drug and alcohol assessment and engage in any recommended treatment prior to any increase in the spend time arrangement with the children.
It is recommended that Mr [Winoc] does not consume alcohol in the 24 hours before spending time with the children or during the time that children are in his care.
It is recommended that once Mr [Winoc] has completed the Men’s Behaviour Change program, the children spend the following time with Mr [Winoc]:-
In week 1 – from Friday 3 pm to Sunday 4 pm
In week 2 – from Wednesday after daycare to Thursday morning before daycare and on a Saturday 10 am to Sunday 4 pm.
Facetime contact every Tuesday night at 4:30 pm.
It is recommended that the above arrangement remain in place for a period of six months before then progressing to the following arrangement:-
In week 1 – from Friday 3 pm to Monday morning before daycare/school
In week 2 – from Wednesday after daycare/school until Friday morning before daycare/school.
It is recommended that the above arrangement remain in place for a period a further period of six months before consideration is given to the children residing with the father on a full-time basis and therefore being a flexible but regular arrangement in place for the children to spend time, including overnights, with the maternal grandmother.
It is recommended that if the Court is compelled by the maternal grandmother’s concerns about the father’s mental health, then there may be some benefit to the father undergoing a mental health assessment and following through with any recommended treatment.
It is recommended that the parties use a parenting application for communication purposes.
It is recommended that whilst the children are transitioning to Mr [Winoc’s] care, the grandmother keep Mr [Winoc] up to date with all medical, educational and social appointments relating to the children, inviting him to attend appointments or, at the very least, providing feedback about the outcomes of such appointments.
It is recommended that neither of the parties denigrate, alienate, abuse, or negatively influence the children against either parent and engage in positive communication about the other parent.
I note that the recommendations commence with a caveat that they are “based on the information gathered in this assessment and unless evidentiary material should indicate otherwise”.
I will come to the child court expert's oral evidence on the significance of the factual findings of family violence I make shortly.

The father gave evidence at his affidavit [129] that he had complied with orders and “have completed hair follicle tests, CDT testing...".[43] The father was ordered to have hair follicle testing for alcohol by the orders of 5 December 2025, with the maternal grandmother ordered to pay, see orders 1 to 4 and notes (a) and (b) of those orders. The father did attend for testing on 18 December 2025, but no testing was undertaken as the payment had not yet been made by the maternal grandmother. There was then a Christmas close down.
I note the history of communications around testing and exhibits R1 and R2. The father attended for testing and would have done the testing if it had been paid for. I accept the father's explanation on this particular issue. I find that the failure to have testing was just due to a miscommunication issue around payment and an inability for the father to arrange to reattend and have the testing done before trial.

FAMILY VIOLENCE AND OTHER FINDINGS

In considering the allegations against the father, which are denied by him, I note that the mother, the alleged victim, and in most cases, the only witness, is not present to give evidence or be questioned. Evidence of her hearsay statements to the maternal grandmother, and to police, her solicitor and others including in police and other documentary material is admitted by reason of section 102NL of the Act, which excludes the hearsay provisions of the Evidence Act 1995 (Cth), must be given such weight, if any, as I consider fit. I note she is also an unavailable witness but do not consider the principles relevantly different.
Given the gravity of the allegations and the consequences of a finding of serious family violence on the outcome of these proceedings, I have in mind in my consideration, section 140(2) of the Evidence Act 1995 (Cth) noting the line of authority from the old Commonwealth decisions in Briginshaw & Briginshaw (1938) 60 CLR 336; [1938] HCA 34 through to the modern interpretation of the current statutory provision.

EARLY FAMILY VIOLENCE

Going back to the start of the relationship, I give some weight to the GG Mental Health Service report of 29 June 2017.[44] I note that the paragraph starting, "'[Ms D] (17 –[...] yrs), self-harm (cutting) after an argument with her boyfriend when he told her to go home and cut herself deeply" could be read, as raised in submissions, with a pause or comma after the word ‘home’, ie it could be read that she said the father told her to go home, and then she went home, and then she cut herself, rather than as him telling her to go home and telling her to cut herself.
However, given the later statement that quote, "'She has been in a relationship for two years with a boy who has recently been violent to her and who tells her to harm herself whenever they argue”, and noting the later proved serious physical violence, I consider this is evidence that the father would tell the mother to self-harm when they argued, and that he did this in the context of his knowledge that she had been gang raped at school, that she said that she was the victim of sexual assault by her own father, and that she was regularly engaging in serious self-harming behaviours requiring hospital presentations.
On balance, I find that the father, in that context, was telling the mother to self-harm when they argued. I cannot make a finding on whether or not the mother specifically self-harmed because the father had told her to on any given occasion. However, the father telling the mother to self-harm, given his knowledge of her mental health status at that time, is evidence of the relationship being founded on a basis of serious, coercive and controlling and manipulative family violence.
This finding is a finding relevant to my assessment of the psychological risks the father poses to the children of engaging in similar manipulative, coercive and controlling verbal violence to them, in addition to the risks, that I will come to, that he poses through possibility of direct physical violence up to the level of strangulation which he imposed upon the mother.
I find that the father's evidence directed to suggesting that the maternal grandmother was a bad mother, and that the mother's mental health problems and self-harming behaviours were a consequence of that, was nothing more than an attempt to falsely smear the maternal grandmother for the purposes of these proceedings, to obtain the orders the father then sought for primary care and sole decision-making authority.

JANUARY 2019: PRIOR STRANGULATION EVENT

For reasons I have given, I am satisfied that the audio evidence of the discussion in exhibit R7 was recorded on January 2019, so that the father's reference to a prior strangulation event must have occurred prior to that date.
Accordingly, I find that there was a strangulation assault, or battery, by the father upon the mother prior to January 2019.
That means that the strangulation event of July 2021 was not the only, or single, or isolated event of strangulation in the relationship.
The father's statement in oral evidence that he could not recall if he had strangled the mother on more than the one occasion on which he was convicted was concerning. One would have thought that the question of whether or not there had been only one occasion on which one strangled one's partner would be relatively straightforward to answer.
I find that the father's oral evidence and pretence of poor memory in relation not this issue was a deliberate fabrication designed to attempt to avoid the finding that there was a prior strangulation event, given that the father was clearly aware of the seriousness of such a finding.
On the issue of the father's history of cannabis and alcohol misuse, the content of this conversation in 2019 is consistent with the evidence led in the maternal grandmother's case that the father has been a serious long-term habitual user of cannabis, and that his cannabis use was a source of conflict in the matrimonial relationship, noting the mother's statement to her solicitor of 14 September 2023, at paragraph [19], that she had seen the father "smoke cannabis daily for years".[45]
Whilst the conversation was in 2019, it is not so inconsistent with the father's statement that he stopped in about 2019, the fact that he was a long-term cannabis user to that stage is one of the pieces of evidence I take into account in finding that, until at least relatively recently with the onset of testing, the father was, as has been submitted, a heavy cannabis user.
Whilst on this topic, I will note that I give no weight to the photo of the father with some kind of pill in exhibit R13. He said it was posted as a joke, and there is no surrounding evidence at all of the father using pills. This is not to minimise the potential issue of cannabis use and a relapse into cannabis use since the father appears to have stopped, but I do not think there is evidence to support any finding of use of pills.

JUNE 2020: PUSHING TO THE GROUND

In relation to the event in June 2020, there was no clear reason for the mother to make a false statement to police in a context where she was unwilling to make a formal complaint, and where there was an intact relationship, if this was considered as the sole piece of evidence, it could be given limited weight.
However, in context of a prior and a subsequent strangulation event, as part of the overall constellation of evidence, and as it is consistent with the overall pattern of evidence, I give weight to the mother's hearsay statement to police as evidence of another specific incident of family violence occurring. While not to the same level as the strangulation events, it speaks to a long and persistent pattern of family violence.

MARCH 2021: SPLIT LIP AND BRUISED GUM

I find that the video was taken on the date indicated by the electronic record.
There is no evidence from any witness, including the father, of any other, "he" who was or could have physically assaulted the mother, causing her such an injury in March 2021, when she was still in some kind of relationship with the father.
I also accept the maternal grandmother's supporting evidence.
In the context of all the evidence, and noting that I accept the maternal grandmother as a substantially honest witness, and in the absence of any indication of another assailant, and given the proven history of two strangulation events, I am satisfied to the relevant evidentiary standard that the mother was stating that the father slapped her across the face and bruised her and split her lip and hurt her gums, as shown on that video.

JULY 2021: STRANGULATION, PUNCHING AND ASSAULT WITH SKATEBOARD

I note that the strangulation, punching and assault with a skateboard in July speaks for itself on [...] July 2021.

JULY 2021: FATHER TAKEN TO GROUND BY PARTERNAL GRANDFATHER

On that same date, I accept the maternal grandmother's oral evidence, which is consistent with the police record apparently provided by her and her husband to police at the time.
This was another occasion, as with the prior strangulation, where the father stated he could not recall the details of what happened.
Having agreed that his father had stopped him, and they had a verbal argument, it is clear he had a recollection of the event. The only thing he could not recall was being taken to the ground. I find it difficult to see how the father could recall the part of the event where his father stopped him and they had a verbal argument, but could not recall the more significant aspect, which is that his own father had to take him to the ground to stop him going to the maternal grandmother's house and doing something potentially violent.
On balance, I consider this to be yet another dishonest statement of lack of memory, intended to avoid having to admit to facts that were not favourable to him.

MAY 2023: COURT AND PLEA OF GUILTY

To the extent the father says that the mother was encouraged by the maternal grandmother and her family to make a report to police, that is clearly correct.
However, the mother was the only one who knew she had video evidence, and who showed it to her family and told them the story, and to that extent it is clear, and I find, that the mother initiated the process leading to the prosecution in circumstances where she was aware of her diagnosis, and I find was concerned about the children's future, for reasons she laid out to her solicitor in her statement of 14 September 2023, which is that she was scared about what would happen to the children if placed in the care of the father.[46] That fear was, given my findings, entirely reasonable and well-founded in fact.
To the extent the father suggested and maintained that he pleaded guilty to avoid the mother being placed under stress and having to give evidence at trial, I reject that. The father could have pleaded guilty at any time prior to trial. He knew what he had done and that he was guilty. He waited until the trial date, [...] before the mother's passing, and required the mother to attend, despite being terminally ill and extremely sick, before entering a plea in the face of the overwhelming video evidence which, once the mother was there to be cross-examined, was incontrovertible evidence which must have resulted in his conviction.
I also find that his suggestion that he pleaded guilty as the mother was battling stage 4 cancer was a calculated lie when told to the child court expert, and repeated in his trial affidavit filed on the first day of the final hearing, to suggest that, to some extent, his plea of guilty was out of compassion for the mother, and so meant to paint him in a good light. His actions in requiring her to attend, at death's door, at court to give evidence was not consistent with that position.
As I said, the father did say, when pressed by me, why he had pleaded guilty, it was “because I had done something horrible and regretted it and did not want her to sit in the courtroom”.
If that had been the father's general position before his oral evidence, including in his interview with the expert and in his trial affidavit, it could have been highly relevant and significant.
However, that statement came only late in cross-examination, in the context of his statements through the Family Report interviews denying any more than one event, and even there denying physical violence, despite not only being aware that there had been physical violence, but that there was video evidence showing it, and was also made in the context of his oral evidence before me, still avoiding by denying memory and denying and minimising the other acts of family violence.
In this context I do not accept there was any real contrition. It was a statement made only in the context of being shown overwhelming evidence that he could not counter.

5 JUNE 2024: CIR INTERVIEWS

In respect of the child impact report on 5 June 2024, the father's report of rare alcohol consumption and denial of alcohol misuse and failure to disclose his recent High Range PCA and other matters in respect to cannabis were, I find, intentional omissions and false statements to promote his case.

20 OCTOBER 2024: FAMILY REPORTER INTERVIEWS

In relation to the father's statements as recorded at paragraphs [27] and [55] of the Family Report, I prefer the child court expert's contemporaneous record of the conversations and her oral evidence confirming their accuracy.
I find that at that time, the father strongly denied any physical violence against the mother when speaking with a child court expert, including in relation to events in which he was convicted, and also denied all other incidents of family violence, at paragraph [57], other than the one incident he was convicted of.
I find that the strong denial of physical violence by the father to the child court expert, including in relation to the matter of which he was convicted, involved a frank lie to the child court expert to advance his case to her in context of the preparation of a report and recommendations to this Court.
It was a bold lie told in the face not only of the conviction for strangulation, but in circumstances where the father was aware there was video evidence which had been provided to police by the mother which proved, beyond any possibility of doubt, his guilt of strangulation as it shows the strangulation and punching and assault occurring.
Further, there was a frank lie that denied other physical violence which I find occurred within the relationship, as just discussed. I find the father told these lies to the child court expert to minimise and avoid responsibility for his violent behaviours in the context where he knew that the nature and extent of any family violence finding could or would affect the child court expert's recommendations to this Court.
I find the father told the child court expert he pleaded guilty, even in part so the mother did not give evidence, was a false statement intended to paint him in a compassionate light.
I find the father's failure to mention his High Range PCA was also an intentional omission designed to minimise the child court expert's knowledge of the true position concerning the father's alcohol use.

FURTHER FAMILY VIOLENCE FINDINGS

I find that the father engaged in serious, long-term, persistent, physical, verbal, and coercive and controlling family violence across the entirety of the parents' relationship, including two proven acts of strangulation, a proven punch to the stomach, assault with a skateboard, a push to the ground, and a hitting of the face causing bruising and a split lip, with evidence of verbal abuse throughout, including of manipulating a vulnerable partner engaged in self-harming by telling her to self-harm when she was in the habit of self-harming and was regularly being admitted to hospital because of that when young.
These findings persuade to me that the family violence was endemic in and throughout the relationship.
It is also clear that, at least in July 2021, X was present, with the strangulation occurring while X was actually in the mother's arms, and I find it is overwhelmingly likely that the children were regularly exposed to significant physical and verbal family violence by the father against the mother.
To the extent to which the father's oral evidence sought to persuade me that he has now done a men's behaviour course and accepted the full nature and extent of his family violence, I find that he has no real contrition for his behaviours despite his one admission of having done something horrible he regretted.
The father’s oral evidence involved him continuing to minimise, and attempting to avoid where possible, responsibility for family violence by saying he either could not remember, for example he said he could not remember if he had strangled the mother more than once. This does not persuade me that he has developed any insight into, or accepted any real responsibility for, this history of significant family violence against the mother.
A behaviour change course is a useful thing only if the consequence of the course is the development of insight, the acceptance of responsibility and, as a consequence of accepting responsibility and developing insight, the behaviour changing, so that the Court can be satisfied that the father is not likely to act that way in future.
His trial affidavit and his oral evidence, except for one or two statements of contrition, are inconsistent with such a finding.
To the contrary, I am satisfied, unfortunately, that the father is likely to continue these behaviours in future, including in the presence of the children, and that he possess a real risk in moments of impulsivity and loss of emotional regulation of committing serious physical violence directed to the children, and also more generally of verbal violence directed to the children, and of making manipulative comments to the children to the extent of family violence.
