CHILD COURT EXPERT’S ORAL EVIDENCE

The child court expert considered the maternal grandmother was the primary attachment figure, especially for Y, who is likely to have no real memories of the mother, and also for X given the history of time spent with her.
That is consistent with it being appropriate for the children to continue to live with the maternal grandmother and also with her exercise of responsibility.
The expert was provided with the video and audio evidence and other evidence referred to already, and as one possible set of assumptions, the findings of family violence which I have made.
The child court expert accepted, in questioning for the father, that she would maintain her opinion and recommendations on the basis on which they are made. However, she said that if she had known of the video evidence of physical violence, including potentially that there were two choking or strangulation events, it would have affected those recommendations.
It is trite that an expert's opinion is only as reliable as the factual assumptions upon which it is based. See Makita & Sprowles [2001] NSWCA 305; (2001) 25 NSWCCR 218; (2001) 52 NSWLR 705 and Dasreef & Hawchar [2011] HCA 21; (2011) 243 CLR 588.
In this case, the expert's recommendations were based on a factual history from the father of no physical violence, and only one event of family violence, which is substantially different to the factual findings concerning family violence which I make.
In relation to choking or strangulation events, as shown in the video, the child court expert said in oral evidence that this was amongst "the most serious" kind of family violence event. In support of that opinion, she referred to social science literature linking choking events to a pattern of escalating violence in future acts up to and including lethal acts. She said that if the Court were to find that there had been two choking or strangulation events, that would significantly elevate her assessment of the father's risk profile, given her view of the seriousness of choking incidents of family violence.
I accept this evidence both as to the weight the court should give to an event of strangulation, and to the fact that a pattern of two proven events of strangulation seriously increases the risk profile of the father.
The child court expert confirmed that on the findings I make, the father had not been honest with her about the level of violence in the relationship. She said she considered this suggested a lack of acceptance of responsibility for, or insight into, or understanding of the impact of, his family violence on the mother, and on the children, by the father when she interviewed him.
The child court expert outlined the impacts on children of exposure to family violence and trauma, in addition to direct physical risks that might exist to the children if spending unsupervised time with the father if he becomes angry or frustrated or emotionally dysregulated, noting the significant physical family violence imposed on the mother in those circumstances.
The child court expert said that exposure to family violence may have affected the children even if is too young to specifically recall the events, and identified the children’s vulnerability to re-traumatisation on those facts, and also raised the risk of violence becoming normalised for a child who experiences it and the associated risks of the children modelling violent behaviour, which might cause them to act in an emotionally dysregulated and violent manner themselves and to start exhibiting similar behaviours, with all the social difficulties and developmental difficulties that would pose to the children.
The child court expert stated that assuming the family violence findings which I in fact make, and assuming the father has not developed insight into or taken responsibility for his behaviours, i.e. that his position is as it was when she interviewed him, so that the court could not be satisfied he would not continue to act in the same ways when spending time with the children, then, in her view, this could be a no-time case, where the children would never see the father again.
If not a no-time case, she said she would be very concerned about any overnight time and very concerned about any unsupervised time. However, she said she was "hesitant to say no time at all" as she did witness a relationship between the children and the father, and she said that the loss of one parent weighed against the complete termination of the existing paternal parental relationship. However, she said that in her view, on my findings of fact, any time would need to be closely monitored.
On balance, she said that depending upon the assessment of risk, she would consider it a supervision case with limited time and less frequent periods, potentially once a month for two hours supervised, and she said that subject to the Court's findings, the supervision could, to be realistic, potentially be done by the paternal grandmother.
When asked in cross examination, on behalf of the father, to assume that the father had in fact developed insight into, and learned significantly from, his past behaviours, and had implemented changes since the Family Report interviews, the child court expert said that in that case, if the court considered there were no further risk issues with the father, then she would stand by her original recommendations.
However, she said the father's denial of physical violence to her was not consistent with the proposition that he had developed insight nor that he had changed, and that, at the time she interviewed him, the father was clearly minimising his responsibility.
She said that if the father was still continuing to minimise now, as he had been when she interviewed him, then the risks the children would be exposed to his family violence behaviours if spending unsupervised time with him had not been effectively addressed by his attendance at the men's behaviour courses, or by any other actions or events.
In relation to the father's attendance on the behaviour change course, the child court expert said it would be up to the court to make an assessment of what the father had learned from the course, and whether he had learned and changed, and whether it was likely the changes would be ongoing, so that the risks had been addressed. However, she agreed that if the father was, to this date, still minimising or not fully acknowledging the true extent of his family violence, then that would suggest that the courses had not, in fact, been effective.
When asked to give an opinion on whether the father had, in fact, developed insight since his interview with her, the child court expert said she was just not in the position to assess that, and this was a factual matter for the court based on the court's assessment of the evidence. That must be so.

FINDINGS ON THE RISKS THE FATHER POSES

The father engaged in very significant physical violence against the mother across the course of their relationship. This included, at least, the two strangulation events which I find occurred, and on which I place very significant weight as indicating that the father is a person who has limited emotional regulation and who believes he is entitled to violently batter others in the most serious way, noting also the punch and skateboard assault. That is also shown by the other family violence events I find occurred.
This, together with the verbal abuse, at least when the mother was young and highly vulnerable, and the coercive and controlling and manipulative behaviour in telling a person he knew who was engaged in self-harming behaviour which caused her to be hospitalised, that she should continue to self-harm when they argued, indicates a very serious long-term pattern of family violence.
The father says that he has changed, but his affidavit provided no evidence of change, at the time of the Family Report interview, the father was denying the family violence and avoiding responsibility.
His oral evidence, in which he maintained many of his denials or feigned an inability to recollect, did nothing to persuade me that he has, in fact, changed.
In that context, his one statement of contrition, that he had done something horrible and regretted it, does not persuade me that he has, in fact, changed, where that came only in oral evidence, in the context of being presented with overwhelming evidence of his violence, while still seeking to avoid responsibility for other acts of violence I find he committed.
I accept the expert's opinion that if his denials of responsibility continue, which I find they do, that this is potentially a no-time case, or a once a month minimal supervised time case.
Unfortunately for the children, I therefore find that if permitted to spend unsupervised time with the father, there is a very high risk that the children would be exposed to physical violence of the kind the father perpetrated against the mother. That risk includes the risk of serious physical violence and injury if the father becomes emotionally dysregulated, for example when faced with the children being defiant or difficult, or for some other reason.
Further, there is a high risk that the children would be exposed to psychological harm from observing the father's dysregulated behaviours, or the risk that he would engage in emotional abuse and verbal abuse, and emotional manipulation of the children, as he did with the mother.
These risks are unacceptable.
It is not possible to state the extent to which the father's family violence behaviours were related to his cannabis and alcohol consumption, noting his denials.
In that context, his apparent reduction or cessation in use does not cause me to be persuaded that it is likely that this has reduced the risks. Nor, in this context, can I know whether he will return to their use.
I have considered whether this should be a no-time case, and certainly cases of this level of family violence would be consistent with a no-time order given the clear level of risk and that the risk the father poses on my findings is, on any view, an unacceptable risk of unsupervised time as the expert indicated and I accept. I did not understand that to be substantially in dispute if I made the findings I make.
I have also considered whether it would be more appropriate to have supervision once a month for two hours in professionally supervised circumstances, as the expert raised.
However, I give significant weight to the fact that both the maternal grandmother and the Independent Children's Lawyer have proposed fortnightly time.
I consider that in making this proposal, the maternal grandmother has acted in a highly child-focused manner, weighing the risks to the children of harm from spending time with the father against the psychological harm they will suffer if, having lost their mother, they were also to lose, effectively, their father.
I also consider the paternal grandmother will act in a very child-focused way, and accept that despite the very significant conflict between the two grandmothers, the paternal grandmother is an appropriate person to supervise.
I note that absent a familial supervisor, it is accepted that the costs and the father’s financial position would probably mean that orders for supervision in a paid context would, in effect, end up being a no-time, or at best a nominal time, order.
As I've said, long-term supervision, and supervision by family members, are generally not appropriate, especially in a case of this level of violence, but each case must be determined on its facts, and I find that where the father is an unacceptable risk of unsupervised time, and the paternal grandmother is the only realistic option for long-term supervision, and where the paternal grandmother has, with her husband, shown herself to be willing to use up to physical restraint of the father to ensure he doesn't commit an act of violence, I am satisfied that she will do all that is required to keep the children safe.
And in those circumstances, I find that the least-worst option for the children is the regime of time proposed by the maternal grandmother and the Independent Children's Lawyer.
I did consider whether it should be slightly less time, but if the children are going to have a relationship, given their young age, seeing him only once every two months will probably be of no utility.

FURTHER ORDERS DELIVERED

In addition to the agreed orders, discussed earlier, I have considered the father's proposal for weekly FaceTime communication as proposed at Orders 5 and 6. On the one hand, there is an argument that, particularly given their young age, frequency is important to meet the goal of maintaining a relationship. A short weekly telephone or FaceTime call will assist that process with no risk of physical violence and a low risk of psychological harm with the maternal grandmother being supervised.
However, the ICL and the maternal grandmother did not propose or support a communication order and, given the very high level of conflict between the maternal grandmother and the father, I find that there is significant potential for a disruption of the relationship between the maternal grandmother and the father by requiring them to be in weekly FaceTime communication, which would result from the maternal grandmother supervising calls.
A disruption in that already fraught relationship would likely impact on the capacity of the maternal grandmother to promote the order she has sought, and so potentially undermine the ability of the children to maintain a regular in-person time with the father. The risk that requiring the maternal grandmother to interact with the father in this way will undermine the functioning of the consent orders for time in my view outweighs the relatively low additional benefit to the children of a weekly phone or FaceTime call, which given their attention spans at their age is likely to be very short anyhow.
The one exception I will make to this, for the children's benefit, is that I will order a FaceTime call on each child's birthday, if they will not be spending time with the father as they may otherwise wonder why he has not made an effort to see them, and on the father's birthday to maintain familial connections.
I will order that the maternal grandmother and paternal grandmother may join the call on the children's birthdays for similar reasons.

LEGISLATIVE FRAMEWORK

The objects of Pt VII of the Act are to (a) ensure that the best interests of the children are met, including by ensuring their safety, and (b) to give effect to the Convention on the Rights of the Child done at New York between 20 November 1989.
Paramount consideration is the best interests of the children pursuant to sections 60CA and 65AA.

PARENTAL RESPONSIBILITY

Parental responsibility is agreed. That is appropriate. The consultation process is appropriate.

Child’s best interests - section 60CC

I note the section 60CC provisions.
The non-hierarchical list includes general considerations. I am required to consider what arrangements would promote the safety, including safety from being subjected to or exposed to family violence, abuse and neglect or other harm, of each child, of each person who is in care of the child, where a person has parental responsibility for the child or children.

General considerations – section 60CC (2)

(a) Safety

Given the grave risks the father poses to the children of exposing them to a level of uncontrolled physical violence he has repeatedly inflicted on the mother, as well as the potential psychological harm from witnessing this and from verbal abuse, the only possible safe course which allows a relationship is for the children to live with the maternal grandmother, as agreed, and for the father to have the limited and supervised time as proposed by the maternal grandmother and Independent Children's Lawyer. The alternative, no time or once a month professionally supervised, are worse alternatives.

(b) Views of child

The children are too young for their views to have been taken by the child court expert, and in any event, given the gravity of the risks, assuming they would want to maintain a relationship with the father, the orders I make are the most time that could safely be ordered in circumstances, and supervision is required to protect them.

(c) Needs of child

The maternal grandmother has demonstrated a good capacity to take care of the developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs of the children.
I note X has had a speech and consequent developmental delays.
As noted in Exhibit R26, the Speech Pathology Initial Assessment Report dated 9 September 2024, under the heading “Hearing”:[48]
Hearing

(d) Parental capacity

In relation to the capacity of each parent who has or is proposed to have parental responsibility for the children to provide for the children's developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs, the maternal grandmother has, again, demonstrated a high capacity, whereas the father, who will not have that responsibility in any event, has limited demonstrated capacity, noting, indeed, his exposure of at least X, and that I find probably both children, to extreme family violence against the mother.
I also note the general evidence of limited experience in caring for the children.

Benefit of relationship

It is agreed that there is a benefit to the children of being able to have a relationship with the father, and the paternal family through him who are significant to the children, to the extent it is safe to do so.
These orders permit such time as is safe, in such circumstances as is safe, and involves the paternal grandmother.
I am satisfied that, although only fortnightly, this will be sufficient time for the children to maintain a relationship with the father across the long term.

(2A) Family Violence

I have considered, as required, family violence, and it is a significant factor in the findings I make and in the orders I make.

MR L

I also note the statements that she made at different times, at different ages, in Exhibits A3 and A4 including:

The father and ICL are concerned that the maternal grandmother appears to have forgiven the maternal grandfather, and reconciled, to the extent that they have a general familial relationship, and that she is treating him as if he is not a risk to children.
The evidence indicates there is some substance to this concern, and that the maternal grandmother no longer sees the maternal grandfather as a risk.
The maternal grandmother says she accepts and appreciates that there is a risk and will abide by the injunction she proposes.
The history of proven family violence by Mr L against the maternal grandmother, which the mother and Ms E were both subjected to, and in respect of which he was convicted and incarcerated, is more than sufficient to justify the ICL and father's concerns, echoed by this court and the injunction, without the need for any finding about the allegations of sexual abuse of the mother.
The consistency of the mother's allegation of sexual abuse as a child also supports the finding of unacceptable risk.
I note that Mr L is not a party, not a witness, has never been charged, and has not been offered an opportunity to defend himself against these historic sex allegations.
I emphasise that this court makes no positive findings in regard to the allegations that Mr L sexually abused the mother. No such findings could be made absent him being given an opportunity to defend them, or the mother being present as a witness.
However, as I have said, the family violence findings reflected in his incarceration are more than sufficient to raise grave concerns.
I note that I advised the maternal grandmother in court that I consider Mr L presents a grave risk to the children, and if she exposes these children to the maternal grandfather in breach of the injunction, it is a matter that should be returned to court, and that it may be necessary to consider whether the children would be more safe in the household of the paternal grandmother, excluding the father, or ultimately, unfortunately, whether DCJ should be notified and asked to intervene.
The maternal grandmother said she understood, and I did not make those comments lightly, nor do I restate them in the judgment lightly. It is no good removing the children from the risks the father poses merely to put them at risk of someone such as the maternal grandfather.

ORDERS SUMMARY

The orders, therefore, will be, as I've indicated, that to all extent parenting orders will be discharged.
The maternal grandmother will have sole parental and decision-making responsibility with the consultation provision proposed.
The children will live with the maternal grandmother.
The children shall spend time with the father, for such time and in such circumstances as agreed in writing between the maternal grandmother and the father. The father should understand that this is not an invitation to him to pester the paternal grandmother. However, should he actually do what he said he is doing, and address his issues, and gain some insight, and start to become a person who can add real benefit to the children's life, then it will be open, under these orders for the maternal grandmother to consider whether more or different time would be appropriate.
However, absent other agreement, the children will spend supervised time with the father, each alternate Sunday from 10 am to 4 pm.
I will let the maternal grandmother pick the first upcoming Sunday, where she starts the orders. In fact, I might give her three Sundays to put in place a regime.
Unless otherwise agreed, the father's time is to be conditional on being supervised by the paternal grandmother. The paternal grandmother must first file an undertaking. No other person is to attend the supervised time without the prior consent of the maternal grandmother. Of course, the maternal grandmother might think it some benefit to the children, and given that the paternal grandfather has been shown to be willing to take action to protect the children, if Mr C Winoc can be present, but that's a matter for the maternal grandmother.
I will make orders in respect of Mother's Day, and also the mother's birthday and the mother's passing date as proposed by the maternal grandmother. However, I will order make-up time on a Sunday.
I will make orders in respect of Christmas Day and children's birthdays.
I will note the father's time shall commence and end at the changeover locations. There is no argument about time.
I am going to make communication orders only for each child's birthday and the father's birthday as indicated earlier.
Communication orders between themselves are basically as proposed by the parties, to be by text generally, telephone in emergencies.
Orders can be provided to schools and the parties can attend as proposed by the maternal grandmother.
The maternal grandmother can provide copies of the orders to people who need to know that she has sole parental responsibility.
I will make restraints, including mutual restraints against denigration and physical discipline, restraints against the father for consuming alcohol or illicit substances for 24 hours prior to or whilst the children are spending time with him, and the order restraining the maternal grandmother from causing the children to come into contact with the maternal grandfather.
I will not name the maternal grandfather in the orders. As a person who has had no opportunity, I do not think the injunction should be publicly stated.
I will make the passport orders that are sought.
Those are my orders and those are the reasons.
If any personal party, including the Independent Children's Lawyer, seeks costs, they can either tell me now or they can file an application in the usual course.
There being no application for costs at the moment, I will make an order that costs can be sought.
The orders will be published.
The judgment will be taken out in due course and settled in accordance with the Rules.
Those are my reasons. I adjourn the proceedings.
